name: W.Badge

values.name: view
values.comment:
W.Badge.view []
        { -- number of unread messages
          content = Just [ H.text "9" ]
        , -- call to action
          children =
            [ W.Button.viewLink []
                { href = "/messages"
                , label = [ H.text "Messages" ]
                }
            ]
        }
values.type: List.List (W.Badge.Attribute msg) -> { content : Maybe.Maybe (List.List (Html.Html msg)), children : List.List (Html.Html msg) } -> Html.Html msg

values.name: viewInline
values.comment: W.Badge.viewInline [] [ H.text "9" ]
values.type: List.List (W.Badge.Attribute msg) -> List.List (Html.Html msg) -> Html.Html msg

name: W.Button

values.name: view
values.type: List.List (W.Button.Attribute msg) -> { label : List.List (Html.Html msg), onClick : msg } -> Html.Html msg

values.name: viewDummy
values.comment:
Useful for HTML/CSS-based triggers.

    W.Modal.viewToggle "toggle-on-click"
        [ W.Button.viewDummy []
            [ H.text "Open or close modal" ]
        ]
values.type: List.List (W.Button.Attribute msg) -> List.List (Html.Html msg) -> Html.Html msg

values.name: viewLink
values.type: List.List (W.Button.Attribute msg) -> { label : List.List (Html.Html msg), href : String.String } -> Html.Html msg

values.name: viewSubmit
values.comment:
When using HTML forms with an `onSubmit` handler we also need a button element with "type" set as "submit".
We shouldn't handle the `onClick` of this button directly otherwise we will two events when submitting.

    H.form
        []
        [ -- ...some fields
        , W.Button.viewSubmit []
            [ H.text "Submit this form!" ]
        ]
values.type: List.List (W.Button.Attribute msg) -> List.List (Html.Html msg) -> Html.Html msg

name: W.Container

values.name: htmlAttrs
values.comment: Note that for `style` attributes you should use the [styleAttrs](https://package.elm-lang.org/packages/uncover-co/elm-widgets/latest/W-Container#styleAttrs) attribute instead.

values.name: styleAttrs
values.comment:
Used to pass in extra styles to your container.

    W.Container.view
        [ W.Container.styles
            [ ( "height", "200px" )
            , ( "position", "relative" )
            ]
        ]
        []

values.name: view

name: W.Styles
comment:
You will need both globalStyles and a theme to get started with elm-widgets.

    view : List (Html msg)
    view =
        [ W.Styles.globalStyles
        , W.Styles.baseTheme

        -- ... your application views
        ]


# Custom Themes

elm-widgets is fully compatible with [elm-theme](https://package.elm-lang.org/packages/uncover-co/elm-theme/latest/). So you can use that package to create and expose new themes to your application.

e.g. This would create a dark-mode ready application based on the user system's settings.

    import Theme
    import W.Styles

    view : List (Html msg)
    view =
        [ W.Styles.globalStyles
        , Theme.globalProviderWithDarkMode
            { light = Theme.lightTheme
            , dark = Theme.darkTheme
            , strategy = Theme.systemStrategy
            }

        -- ... your application views
        ]


# API

@docs globalStyles, baseTheme

values.name: labelClass
values.comment:
Pass in extra classes for a column label.
This can be useful for when you want to conditionally hide sorting icons unless the user is hovering a table header.

An example using tailwindcss:

    W.Table.string
        [ W.Table.labelClass "group"
        , W.Table.labelRight
            [ div
                [ class "opacity-0 group-hover:opacity-100" ]
                [ .. ]
            ]
        ]
        { .. }

values.name: string
